speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Steinberg, Herman. In Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where is he standing? Which-- he's the man on the-- which side?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1933, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia. My brother is behind the bride. His name was Mendel, Menachem Mendel. He-- he was born in Czechoslovakia. And this is taken in Montreal, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's standing next to your brother, on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next? My sister. She is wearing dark clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in 19-- 1967. It's now already 7, but eh, it's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter. She is in the right side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is my son-in-law, Stuart.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to Peggy is my grandson, Daniel. And it's my son-in-law, Brian. And that's my son, Joe. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I don't see [INAUDIBLE], I don't know, Shannon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the left? Or on the right?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the far left, who's-- who's standing next to your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my son is Shannon, my grandson, Shannon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and starting from the bottom right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Daniel, my grandson. Then it's my daughter, Ruth. And then my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is my sister, Chaia Choch. And then is my daughter-in-law, Eva Kleinman. And-- and the next is Ariel, my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's taken in-- three years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken here, in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say-- OK, who is in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I say? That's me, Gizella Kleinman. That's Stephanie, my granddaughter. And that's my husband, Israel Kleinman.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken here, in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my granddaughter, Jamie. And it's taken in 19-- '96.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my granddaughter, Stephanie. This is both her husband, Yeshua, and the baby is Yael Devorah. And it's taken about two months? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year would that be?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kleinman, can you please introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the right is my husband, Israel Kleinman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Next--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my son-in-law, Dr. Brian Tabak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next is my daughter Peggy Tabak.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandson. And next is Daniel--

speaker: interviewee
text: There are more family members. But of course, they're not here. They live in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Kleinman, would you like to say anything?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about your experiences about your wife, or how you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in my place. I have a store with my brother, [INAUDIBLE]. And she come to fix something. So that's when we met. That was-- since that time we was going together. And we come to-- to Germany together, and we married in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they just celebrated their 50th wedding anniversary last year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we have the 50th anniversary.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's it. I was a survivor, but from [NON-ENGLISH] survivors. So one of them. I-- I was in his [? ambassador. ?] So--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to say it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell a little more, or elaborate on that if you'd like. He-- he has a cousin who actually pretended he was not Jewish. And he headed up the Red Cross in Hungary for-- for I don't know how many years. And my father-in-law was in an [NON-ENGLISH]. And basically, near the end of the war, when they were taking the Jews from Hungary away, his cousin tried to get him to be rescued by Raoul Wallenberg, told him to come and meet at the Swedish embassy, to try to get in there. And he came. And he's a common shy guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't do much. He stood in line. And it was all the way around the block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so his cousin, Armand [NON-ENGLISH], came and grabbed him, and pushed him into the front of the line, and managed to get him in there. And then he was in the embassy. And apparently the Nazi took potshots through windows, trying to kill some of the people who were there. I'm not sure how long he was there, but ultimately--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months. And ultimately, he was fortunate enough the war was over and survived because of Raoul Wallenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: His brother's here in Los Angeles. I have an older brother. His name is Herman Kleinman. He was in-- also in the-- in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And he went out in 1942. And he survived. He come home in 1943, by the end. And the Russian was going back. So he come to-- to come where we are-- was living. And another two, three, kilometers farther, he survived one night. Then the Russian-- then he got free. So he is here in Los Angeles. He is living in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you would like to say to your family, your grandchildren, great grandchildren, or anybody that might be watching this particular testimony about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope that it's never going to reoccur, this-- this kind of a thing, like what the Germans did to-- to our brothers, sisters and parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would anybody else like to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing is just my parents were fortunate enough, they were from Poland. And they were on the Western border close to Russia. Excuse me, Eastern border, close to Russia. And when the Germans invaded, at that time, they were initially captured as well. But then what happened was, when the Germans made a pact, the Nazis made a pact with the Russians, that little portion of land was given back to the Russians. They held it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that point my parents were able to get away with-- they were not married. And then they got married shortly after. And-- and their parent-- my father's parents were able to go way with them as well, and survived initially, but died for other causes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But most of the other family did not want to go and not want the be displaced. And many of them died. My mother only had two sisters who survived of a family of about eight. And my father had one brother who survived and one sister that survived. The brother passed away in [NON-ENGLISH] since then. And the-- the sister's still alive and living in Argentina. They're scattered all over the world. All over the world. And the only thing that I-- because personally, I have always been very touched by the whole thing, as a-- survive-- I mean, I was born just at the end of the war. I never really experienced any of it. And my parents wouldn't tell us too much in the way of stories about their experiences or anything. But as I got a little older and read about it, I always-- just always moved me. And I still I couldn't go watch 'Schindler's List' in the theater. And I don't-- I don't see that in my kids. I think that-- they don't have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I watched it twice. and I tell them to watch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that. I think it's getting more distant. And they-- they can-- they feel, you know, empathy with it. But they-- but it just doesn't touch the same kind of chord. I have a lot of difficulty watching those kind of movies and stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peggy, would you like to say anything? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in-- in one of the-- in Germany. And of course, I don't remember anything. When I was 1-year-old we came to Montreal. And my parents never really told us much. It's just happening now, that you know, we're hearing the stories, and which are such atrocities, it's so hard to believe. And she's suffering. They're both suffering. And we love them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would either of you like to say anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, of course, were born way after the war. We hear about it a lot more though, as children, unlike my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: They teach it to us in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have it in school. I remember in elementary school we had a Holocaust class. And we still learn about that stuff. And then I'd come home. And whenever my grandparents were here, I'd ask them about certain stories. And they tell me pretty much everything. And it's like-- it's like, a first-hand experience when you're told the stories, and stuff like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you hope never going to come again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much for being here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was hard, but I made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Spielberg, for making these things so that the world can remember.
